claim_text: The most commonly used IQ metrics to evaluate the IQ of MR reconstruction magnitude images are normalized root mean square error (NRMSE), peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), and structural similarity index measure (SSIM).5

sub_claims.text: chen2022 supports that NRMSE/SSIM/PSNR are the most commonly used IQ metrics in MR reconstruction.

evidence.section: V. Meta-Analysis Results § C. Evaluation Metrics
evidence.snippet: To compare the reconstructed images with the ground truth, most studies reported the structural similarity index measure (SSIM) and the peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) [see Fig. 4(b)]. Fewer used NRMSE, MSE, and the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). NRMSE and SSIM became more popular over time, whereas PSNR and NMSE decreased in popularity (see Table 5 in the Supplementary Material).

evidence.section: V. Meta-Analysis Results § C. Evaluation Metrics
evidence.snippet: Thus, quantitative metrics, such as SSIM, PSNR, and NRMSE, were the most prevalent.

evidence.section: V. Meta-Analysis Results § C. Evaluation Metrics
evidence.snippet: Despite the difficulty of interpreting the qualitative metrics, quantitative metrics, e.g., SSIM, PSNR, and MSE, were not linearly dependent.

sub_claims.nuance: Chen2022 explicitly states 'quantitative metrics, such as SSIM, PSNR, and NRMSE, were the most prevalent' — the set of three metrics matches the manuscript's claim exactly. Minor reordering: chen2022 ranks SSIM/PSNR more frequent than NRMSE, while the manuscript orders 'NRMSE, PSNR, SSIM'; this does not affect the substantive claim about which three metrics are most common. Scope nuance: chen2022's surveyed literature is deep-learning CS-MRI specifically rather than all MR reconstruction broadly, but the metric-prevalence finding is the natural support for the manuscript's framing.

section_checklist.section: V. Meta-Analysis Results § C. Evaluation Metrics

attestation.indirect_attribution_check: Chen2022 reports its own meta-analysis findings on metric prevalence across the surveyed CS-MRI literature; it does not credit another primary source for this claim. The finding is original to chen2022's systematic review/meta-analysis.
attestation.out_of_context_check: Context matches: chen2022 is reporting on IQ metrics used to evaluate MR reconstruction (specifically deep-learning-based CS-MRI), which aligns with the manuscript's framing of 'IQ metrics to evaluate the IQ of MR reconstruction'. Minor nuance: chen2022's scope is deep-learning CS-MRI specifically, not all MR reconstruction approaches broadly, but the claim is about IQ metrics commonly used in the MR reconstruction literature, which chen2022 directly addresses.
